message.content:
Research online about:

1. "State machines vs React hooks architecture" - find articles explaining why state machines are superior for complex apps
2. "Redux vs useState" - understand the tradeoffs (similar philosophy to @belt/tools vs hooks)
3. "XState philosophy" or "finite state machines web apps" - understand the "state-first" philosophy
4. Look for best practices on "single source of truth" state management
5. Find criticisms of hooks for complex state (prop drilling, scattered state, no time-travel debugging)

This will help me document WHY we're blocking hooks and articulate the architectural philosophy clearly in the plan.

content.text: I'll research these topics to understand the architectural philosophy around state machines vs React hooks for complex applications.

input.query: state machines vs React hooks architecture complex applications
Redux vs useState tradeoffs state management
XState finite state machines web applications philosophy
single source of truth state management best practices
React hooks problems complex state prop drilling time-travel debugging

Links: [{"title":"useState vs. Redux: Choosing the Right State Management Tool for Your React Application | by Suren Poghosyan | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@surenpoghosian/usestate-vs-redux-choosing-the-right-state-management-tool-for-your-react-application-e1a00e8e6ac8"},{"title":"State Management Beyond useState: Context API vs Redux vs Zustand vs Jotai | by Karan | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@karanssoni2002/state-management-beyond-usestate-context-api-vs-redux-vs-zustand-vs-jotai-ee6627b45d1b"},{"title":"React 101: Comparing useState, Context, and Redux | Fishtank","url":"https://www.getfishtank.com/insights/comparing-usestate-context-and-redux-in-react"},{"title":"Organizing State | Redux","url":"https://redux.js.org/faq/organizing-state"},{"title":"React State Management: When to Use useState vs. Redux | by Mayank Jain | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@mayank2000jain/react-state-management-when-to-use-usestate-vs-redux-e556886de4cb"},{"title":"Bridging the Gap between React's useState, useReducer, and Redux | Strings and Things","url":"https://leewarrick.com/blog/a-guide-to-usestate-and-usereducer/"},{"title":"Should I use Redux or React UseState Hook for state management? - Quora","url":"https://www.quora.com/Should-I-use-Redux-or-React-UseState-Hook-for-state-management"},{"title":"How to Manage State in Your Applications – useState() vs Redux","url":"https://www.freecodecamp.org/news/usestate-vs-redux-state-management/"},{"title":"React state management - useReducer vs Redux | Saeloun Blog","url":"https://blog.saeloun.com/2023/08/31/react-state-management-usereducer-vs-redux/"},{"title":"Redux vs React State System","url":"https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/redux-vs-react-state-system-parisa-darkhal"}]

Here's a comprehensive overview of the key tradeoffs between Redux and useState for state management:

## Scope & Use Cases

useState is best for local, component-specific state, while Redux handles global state across your entire app.(no content)Redux helps keep things organized in large apps or when you need to share the same state between many components.

## Setup & Complexity

useState is simple and quick to set up, while Redux comes with more setup and is better suited for larger projects.(no content)Redux has boilerplate-heavy setup (though the newer Redux Toolkit simplifies this).

## Advantages

**useState Benefits:**
useState is simple, lightweight, and perfect for managing local state within a single component or a small app.

**Redux Benefits:**
Redux's structure makes state changes predictable, which is great for debugging and scaling your app.(no content)Debugging is easier with Redux DevTools.(no content)Redux supports middleware like Redux Thunk or Redux Saga for handling asynchronous operations.

## When to Use Each

Use useState for simplicity and speed in smaller projects, like forms or toggles. Reach for Redux when your app scales up, demanding shared state across components, like in e-commerce or social platforms.(no content)useState does not provide a global or centralized way to manage state across components, making it harder to share and synchronize data, and may cause prop drilling issues if you need to pass data between distant components.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Learn useContext in React Like a Pro - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/chintanonweb/say-goodbye-to-prop-drilling-learn-usecontext-in-react-like-a-pro-451e"},{"title":"Understanding Prop Drilling and How to Solve It - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/paulocappa/understanding-prop-drilling-and-how-to-solve-it-2bnd"},{"title":"How to Avoid Prop Drilling Using Hooks and Context API in React","url":"https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/how-avoid-prop-drilling-using-hooks-context-api-react-alex-lomia"},{"title":"A better way of solving prop drilling in React apps - LogRocket Blog","url":"https://blog.logrocket.com/solving-prop-drilling-react-apps/"},{"title":"Props Drilling in React: What It Is and How to Avoid It","url":"https://xlearnerdev.com/props-drilling-in-react-what-it-is-how-to-avoid-it/"},{"title":"State Management in React: Redux vs Context API vs Zustand — Which Should You Choose? | by Ranamzulqarnain | Jan, 2026 | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@ranamzulqarnain1/state-management-in-react-redux-vs-context-api-vs-zustand-which-should-you-choose-328862e5ae3d"},{"title":"Meet Reactime — A Time-traveling State Debugger for React | by Yujin | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@yujinkay/meet-reactime-a-time-traveling-state-debugger-for-react-24f0fce96802"},{"title":"Prop drilling in react & How to avoid it? | by Khushboo | Medium","url":"https://khushboo11.medium.com/prop-drilling-in-react-how-to-avoid-it-f1c6fae46d15"},{"title":"Avoid Prop Drilling Chaos: How React Hooks & Context Boundaries Simplify State Management in 2025 | by Adnan Hamisi | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@ahamisi777/avoid-prop-drilling-chaos-how-react-hooks-context-boundaries-simplify-state-management-in-2025-ff79c51845d2"},{"title":"A Guide to React State Management Libraries and Patterns | Tapflare","url":"https://tapflare.com/articles/react-state-management-libraries-guide"}]

I've completed the web search for your query about React hooks problems, complex state management, prop drilling, and time-travel debugging. Here's a summary of the key findings:

## Main Topics Found:

**Prop Drilling Issues:**
Prop drilling refers to the process of passing data through multiple layers of components via props.(no content)When you pass data through many nested components that don't necessarily need it — just to reach one that does — it clutters your code, bloats component APIs, and makes future updates painful.

**Complex State Management Solutions:**
- For complex state management with features like middlewares, immutability, and time-travel debugging, Redux is a better fit.
- The useReducer hook is useful for managing complex states in React functional components and can be combined with the Context API to avoid prop drilling.

**Time-Travel Debugging:**
Reactime is an open-source Chrome developer tool designed to help developers debug React applications by tracking and visualizing state changes, and it allows developers to revert to any previously recorded state. Additionally, Redux's strengths include a strong ecosystem and time-travel debugging.

**Alternative Libraries:**
Zustand has surged in popularity as a tiny, hook-based state library that provides a global store without the ceremony of Redux.

The search results provide comprehensive information about addressing these React development challenges through various state management approaches and debugging tools.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"What is a Single Source of Truth (SSoT)? Definition, Benefits, and Best Practices","url":"https://www.getguru.com/reference/single-source-of-truth"},{"title":"What is a Single Source of Truth (SSOT) | MuleSoft","url":"https://www.mulesoft.com/resources/esb/what-is-single-source-of-truth-ssot"},{"title":"Single Source of Truth Guide | Lucid","url":"https://lucid.co/blog/single-source-of-truth-guide"},{"title":"Single Source of Truth [+ How to Build One] | The Workstream","url":"https://www.atlassian.com/work-management/knowledge-sharing/documentation/building-a-single-source-of-truth-ssot-for-your-team"},{"title":"Single source of truth - Wikipedia","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single_source_of_truth"},{"title":"Single Source of Truth (SSOT): What It Is and Why It Matters","url":"https://www.thoughtspot.com/data-trends/best-practices/single-source-of-truth"},{"title":"What Is a Single Source of Truth & How to Build It","url":"https://strapi.io/blog/what-is-single-source-of-truth"},{"title":"Best Practices for Any Single Source of Truth","url":"https://facilityone.com/hospitality-blog/ssot"},{"title":"Single Source of Truth (SSOT): Definition and Concepts - KPI Fire","url":"https://www.kpifire.com/blog/ssot-single-source-of-truth/"},{"title":"Master Data Management - A Single Source of Truth ➤","url":"https://www.stibosystems.com/blog/how-to-enable-a-single-source-of-truth-with-mdm-and-integration"}]

I've performed a web search on single source of truth state management best practices. Here's what I found:

## Definition

A single source of truth (SSOT) is a data management principle that mandates using one centralized, authoritative system to maintain consistency and accuracy across an organization.

## Key Benefits

A single source of truth reinforces confidence that everyone in your business is looking at the same data during their decision-making process. Additionally, implementing SSOT streamlines data management, reduces search time, and fosters cross-functional teamwork by ensuring everyone works from the same accurate information.

## Implementation Best Practices

Several key best practices have been identified:

1. **Data Governance**: Establish clear data governance policies and procedures, and define roles and responsibilities for data ownership, stewardship, and quality control.

2. **Change Management**: Establish a robust change management process to govern any modifications to the SSOT, with changes carefully documented, tested, and communicated to all team members, plus implement version control to track and manage changes over time.

3. **Selective Data Integration**: Be selective and try to pull in your most important data first—you don't have to connect it all at once, and keeping formatting consistent from the beginning will help establish the habit on your team.

4. **Data Quality**: Every department should develop standard operating procedures (SOPs) for updating and uploading data to the platform, and consider using automated data validation tools to set specific rules for files.

5. **User Adoption**: User adoption is critical to the success of a master data management and single source of truth implementation, with involvement of end-users early in the process to ensure the system meets their needs.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"xstate - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/xstate"},{"title":"XState - JavaScript State Machines and Statecharts","url":"https://xstate.js.org/"},{"title":"Empowering Frontend Applications with Finite State Machines and XState | Devspedia","url":"https://devspedia.com/empowering-frontend-applications-with-fsm-and-xstate/"},{"title":"GitHub - statelyai/xstate: Actor-based state management & orchestration for complex app logic.","url":"https://github.com/statelyai/xstate"},{"title":"Hello XState Part 1: Learning state machines for frontend web development - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/ekafyi/hello-xstate-learning-state-machines-for-frontend-web-development-5bin"},{"title":"Creating State Machines in JavaScript with XState | by Dor Nisim | Medium","url":"https://nisimdor.medium.com/creating-state-machines-in-javascript-with-xstate-21e1599521be"},{"title":"A Complete Introduction to State Machines in JavaScript","url":"https://jonbellah.com/articles/intro-state-machines"},{"title":"Don’t use XState (at least, not with React) | by Brian Boyko | Medium","url":"https://brianboyko.medium.com/dont-use-xstate-at-least-not-with-react-cf46a5f6212b"},{"title":"XState","url":"https://stately.ai/docs/xstate"},{"title":"Guidelines for State Machines and XState | Kyle Shevlin","url":"https://kyleshevlin.com/guidelines-for-state-machines-and-xstate/"}]

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about XState, finite state machines, and their application to web development:

## What is XState?

XState is a state management and orchestration solution for JavaScript and TypeScript apps that has zero dependencies and is useful for frontend and backend application logic. It uses event-driven programming, state machines, statecharts, and the actor model to handle complex logic in predictable, robust, and visual ways.

## The Philosophy Behind State Machines

Statecharts are a formalism for modeling stateful, reactive systems that is useful for declaratively describing the behavior of your application, from the individual components to the overall application logic.(no content)A State Machine (also known as Finite State Machine) is a pattern—a mathematical model of computation with implementations across various science and programming fields.(no content)An FSM is an abstract machine that can be in exactly one of a finite number of states at any given time, can change from one state to another in response to external inputs (called a transition), and is defined by a list of its states, initial state, and the conditions for each transition.

## Benefits for Web Applications

Finite state machines provide a robust framework to manage complex application states, and by leveraging XState, developers can build frontend systems that are predictable, testable, and easier to maintain.(no content)State machines can serve as the "single source of truth" for application states and can be used as the basis for discussions—once signed off on by all stakeholders, developers and designers can proceed with implementation.

## Framework Agnostic Approach

XState is completely framework agnostic.(no content)It integrates well with React, Vue, Svelte, and other frameworks and can be used in the frontend, backend, or wherever JavaScript runs.

Avoiding common pitfalls through… | by Jon Mulligan | Pairs Engineering | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/eureka-engineering/react-without-the-state-machine-ce4fd25a6180"},{"title":"Selecting a finite state machine library for React - Rainforest QA Blog | Software Testing Guides","url":"https://www.rainforestqa.com/blog/selecting-a-finite-state-machine-library-for-react"},{"title":"State Machines in React","url":"https://mastery.games/post/state-machines-in-react/"},{"title":"7 Best React State Management Libraries for Efficient State Handling - ITSharkz","url":"https://itsharkz.com/blog/7-best-react-state-management-libraries-for-efficient-state-handling/"},{"title":"State Management in React – Hooks, Context API and Redux - GeeksforGeeks","url":"https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/reactjs/state-management-in-react-hooks-context-api-and-redux/"},{"title":"React State Management in 2024 - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/nguyenhongphat0/react-state-management-in-2024-5e7l"},{"title":"React State Management: React Hooks vs Redux.io","url":"https://tsh.io/blog/react-state-management-react-hooks-vs-redux/"}]

Based on the web search results, here's a comprehensive comparison of state machines versus React hooks architecture for complex applications:

## State Machines vs React Hooks for Complex Applications

### React Hooks Architecture

React has built-in features for state management, including the useState and useReducer Hooks, as well as the Context API.(no content)Most of the time, you will want to use both to address global state management and scalability challenges in complex commercial web applications.

However, there are limitations. While state management solutions like useState and useReducer make sense when the scope of the state is just a single component, or when the state is supposed to be accessed in a downstream component, these restrictions make it difficult to use these solutions in larger applications where the order of creation and consumption of state is not defined.

### State Machines Approach

A finite state machine can be in only one of a finite number of states at any given time. As an application is interacted with, events cause it to change state.(no content)Every React component is actually an "implicit state machine" — cobbled together based on the component's spread out logic. But a component driven by an explicit state machine is even better. The state machine is the brain of the component.

### Practical Benefits for Complex Applications

State machines excel in managing intricate UI flows. State machines are a great candidate for describing all possible states of the editor and transitions between them. These states and transitions are currently somewhat scattered throughout the codebase and getting an overall picture of what happens when and what are all the possible combinations requires a lot of jumping around. Making a mental map of all the possibilities is therefore difficult and that also means it's difficult to onboard new developers. The ability to quickly analyze the application states and transitions between them can also be beneficial while collaborating with project managers, designers, customer support representatives or anyone else interested in the product functionality. A diagram of the state machine can be a great addition to feature documentation.

### When to Use Each

Redux (especially with Redux Toolkit) remains a powerful choice for complex applications requiring advanced state management features.(no content)Advanced solutions like state machines or atomic state management libraries such as Recoil and Jotai, empower developers to architect applications with optimized performance and maintainability.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: Now let me compile this research into a comprehensive synthesis document:

input.content:
# State Machines vs React Hooks: Architectural Philosophy & Research

**Research Date:** January 14, 2026
**Purpose:** Document the architectural rationale for blocking hooks in complex applications and using state machines as the single source of truth.

---

## Executive Summary

State machines (FSMs) and statecharts represent a fundamentally different architectural philosophy from React hooks for managing complex application state. While hooks are appropriate for local, component-specific state, they create systematic problems in applications with complex, interconnected state transitions. State machines solve these by:

1. **Centralizing state logic** into explicit, visualizable machines
2. **Preventing invalid state combinations** through finite state constraints
3. **Enabling time-travel debugging** and state reproducibility
4. **Providing a single source of truth** for all application behavior
5. **Facilitating cross-team communication** (developers, designers, stakeholders)

---

## 1. State Machines vs React Hooks: Core Philosophical Differences

### React Hooks Philosophy
- **Implicit state machines**: Each component's state logic is scattered across multiple hooks, effects, and conditional branches
- **Local by default**: Each hook manages state independently without global coordination
- **Coupled to components**: State logic is tightly bound to React component lifecycle
- **Difficult mental model**: No centralized picture of "what states can the app be in?" or "what are all valid transitions?"

### State Machines Philosophy
- **Explicit state machines**: All possible states and transitions are declared upfront
- **Centralized**: Single machine is the authoritative source for application behavior
- **Framework agnostic**: State logic is independent of UI framework (React, Vue, Svelte, etc.)
- **Clear mental model**: Developers and stakeholders can visualize the entire state space

**Sources:**
- [The modern guide to React state patterns - LogRocket Blog](https://blog.logrocket.com/modern-guide-react-state-patterns/)
- [State Machines in React](https://mastery.games/post/state-machines-in-react/)

---

## 2. Why Hooks Fail at Scale: The Real Problems

### Problem 1: Scattered State Logic (No Single Picture)
When using hooks, state logic is distributed across:
- Multiple `useState()` calls in a component
- Logic in `useEffect()` hooks
- Conditional branches in event handlers
- State synchronization scattered across multiple components

**The Problem**: To understand what states the application can be in, you must:
1. Read every component's state definitions
2. Trace all useEffect dependencies
3. Map out all possible conditional branches
4. Mentally model all edge cases

This is exponentially harder as complexity grows.

**State Machine Alternative**: All states and transitions are declared in one place:
```typescript
const machine = createMachine({
  id: 'fleetApp',
  initial: 'loading',
  states: {
    loading: { on: { IMPORT_COMPLETE: 'viewing' } },
    viewing: { on: { DRAG_START: 'dragging', ZOOM: 'viewing' } },
    dragging: { on: { DROP: 'validating', CANCEL: 'viewing' } },
    validating: { on: { VALID: 'viewing', INVALID: 'error' } },
    error: { on: { RETRY: 'viewing' } },
  },
});
```

Developers, designers, and stakeholders can immediately see all 5 states and all valid transitions.

**Sources:**
- [React Without the State Machine](https://medium.com/eureka-engineering/react-without-the-state-machine-ce4fd25a6180)
- [Selecting a finite state machine library for React](https://www.rainforestqa.com/blog/selecting-a-finite-state-machine-library-for-react)

### Problem 2: Prop Drilling & Hidden Dependencies
When state is local to components, passing state between distant components requires:
1. Lifting state up to common ancestor
2. Threading state through all intermediate components as props
3. Creating hidden dependencies that break when component structure changes

**The Result**: Props become cluttered, intermediate components become "pass-through" components, and refactoring the component tree becomes dangerous.

**State Machine Alternative**: All state lives in one machine. Components query what they need. No prop threading required. Refactoring component structure has no impact on state flow.

**Sources:**
- [Understanding Prop Drilling and How to Solve It - DEV Community](https://dev.to/paulocappa/understanding-prop-drilling-and-how-to-avoid-it-2bnd)
- [Redux vs useState: Choosing the Right State Management Tool](https://medium.com/@surenpoghosian/usestate-vs-redux-choosing-the-right-state-management-tool-for-your-react-application-e1a00e8e6ac8)

### Problem 3: Invalid State Combinations
With hooks, it's possible to create impossible states:

```typescript
// Bad: Can this component be uploading AND showing an error?
const [isLoading, setIsLoading] = useState(false);
const [error, setError] = useState(null);
const [data, setData] = useState(null);
// What happens if all three are "true"? Is the state valid?
```

**The Result**: Bugs in edge cases where two state variables get out of sync.

**State Machine Alternative**: Only one state is active at any time. Invalid combinations are mathematically impossible:

```typescript
const machine = createMachine({
  initial: 'idle',
  states: {
    idle: {},
    loading: {},
    success: {},
    error: {},
  },
});
// State machine guarantees only ONE of these can be active
```

**Sources:**
- [React State Management: When to Use useState vs. Redux](https://medium.com/@mayank2000jain/react-state-management-when-to-use-usestate-vs-redux-e556886de4cb)

### Problem 4: No Time-Travel Debugging
With hooks, debugging complex state flows is painful:
- No record of state transitions over time
- Hard to reproduce bugs (require exact sequence of user actions)
- No way to "rewind" and see what changed
- Debugging across multiple components is a guessing game

**State Machine Alternative**: Every state transition is explicit and recorded. Time-travel debugging tools can:
- Rewind to any previous state
- Replay user actions
- Inspect what caused each transition
- Reproduce bugs deterministically

**Sources:**
- [Meet Reactime — A Time-traveling State Debugger for React](https://medium.com/@yujinkay/meet-reactime-a-time-traveling-state-debugger-for-react-24f0fce96802)
- [Redux vs useState: Choosing the Right State Management Tool](https://medium.com/@surenpoghosian/usestate-vs-redux-choosing-the-right-state-management-tool-for-your-react-application-e1a00e8e6ac8)

---

## 3. State Machines as "Single Source of Truth"

### The SSOT Principle
A Single Source of Truth (SSOT) ensures that:
1. **One authoritative definition** of state exists
2. **All components derive from that definition** (never duplicate state)
3. **State changes are centralized** (all mutations go through the machine)
4. **Everyone sees the same data** (no stale state in different components)

### Why SSOT Matters in Complex Apps
When state is scattered across components:
- Component A has state version v1.0
- Component B has state version v0.9
- They show conflicting information to the user
- Debugging becomes a nightmare

**State Machine as SSOT**: The machine is the single authoritative source. All components subscribe to the same machine and derive UI from that one state.

**Sources:**
- [What is a Single Source of Truth (SSOT)?](https://www.getguru.com/reference/single-source-of-truth)
- [Single Source of Truth Guide | Lucid](https://lucid.co/blog/single-source-of-truth-guide)

---

## 4. XState Philosophy: Framework-Agnostic State Orchestration

### What is XState?
XState is a JavaScript library implementing statecharts (an extension of FSMs) that provides:
- **Event-driven programming**: State changes triggered by explicit events
- **Hierarchical states**: Nested state machines for complex scenarios
- **Parallel states**: Multiple independent state regions
- **Actions & guards**: Side effects and conditional transitions
- **Visual representation**: State machines can be diagrammed and understood by stakeholders

### Core XState Philosophy
> "Finite state machines provide a robust framework to manage complex application states, and by leveraging XState, developers can build frontend systems that are predictable, testable, and easier to maintain."

Key benefits:
1. **Predictability**: All possible states are known in advance
2. **Testability**: State transitions are deterministic (given event E in state S, result is always deterministic)
3. **Maintainability**: Single mental model of application behavior
4. **Visualization**: Machines can be diagrammed and discussed with non-technical stakeholders
5. **Framework agnostic**: Works with React, Vue, Svelte, or standalone

### XState as Stakeholder Communication Tool
> "State machines can serve as the 'single source of truth' for application states and can be used as the basis for discussions—once signed off on by all stakeholders, developers and designers can proceed with implementation."

This is particularly valuable for complex applications like MMS where:
- Product managers need to understand all possible workflows
- Designers need to know what UX states exist
- QA needs to verify all state combinations
- Developers need a clear specification

**Sources:**
- [XState - JavaScript State Machines and Statecharts](https://xstate.js.org/)
- [Empowering Frontend Applications with Finite State Machines and XState](https://devspedia.com/empowering-frontend-applications-with-fsm-and-xstate/)
- [Guidelines for State Machines and XState](https://kyleshevlin.com/guidelines-for-state-machines-and-xstate/)

---

## 5. Redux vs useState: The Deeper Lesson

### Why Redux Won't Die
Redux introduced patterns that are now standard in complex apps:
1. **Centralized store**: Single source of truth
2. **Predictable mutations**: Actions describe what happened, reducers describe how state changes
3. **Middleware ecosystem**: Handles async operations, logging, analytics
4. **Developer tools**: Redux DevTools for time-travel debugging
5. **Organizational clarity**: Clear data flow (action → reducer → state → UI)

### Why useState Alone Is Insufficient
While useState is simple for local state, it fails at scale because:
1. **No centralization**: Each component manages its own state
2. **Prop drilling**: Passing state through 5+ levels of components
3. **Unclear data flow**: Hard to trace where state changes originate
4. **No debugging tools**: No time-travel, no middleware, no tooling
5. **Invalid state combos**: Multiple booleans can create impossible states

### The Synthesis
The best pattern for complex apps combines:
- **useState**: Only for truly local UI state (form input, hover state, etc.)
- **State machines**: For application-level state and complex workflows
- **Redux/Zustand**: Alternative to state machines if you prefer action-reducer pattern

**Sources:**
- [useState vs. Redux: Choosing the Right State Management Tool](https://medium.com/@surenpoghosian/usestate-vs-redux-choosing-the-right-state-management-tool-for-your-react-application-e1a00e8e6ac8)
- [State Management Beyond useState: Context API vs Redux vs Zustand vs Jotai](https://medium.com/@karanssoni2002/state-management-beyond-usestate-context-api-vs-redux-vs-zustand-vs-jotai-ee6627b45d1b)
- [React State Management: When to Use useState vs. Redux](https://medium.com/@mayank2000jain/react-state-management-when-to-use-usestate-vs-redux-e556886de4cb)

---

## 6. Architectural Rules for MMS: Why Hooks Are Blocked

### Rule: No Application-Level State in Hooks

**Definition**: "Application-level state" is any state that:
- Affects multiple components or features
- Persists across component tree re-renders
- Is queried by 2+ components
- Represents business logic (fleet data, PM history, schedule changes)
- Requires time-travel debugging or audit trail

**All application-level state MUST use @belt/tools machines.**

### Exceptions: When Hooks Are OK
Hooks are acceptable ONLY for:
1. **Temporary UI state**: `<input>` focus, hover state, animation phase
2. **Local component state**: Button disabled state, local form data before submission
3. **Derived UI state**: Layout dimensions, computed display values

**Rule of thumb**: If you'd want to debug it or it affects another component, use a machine. If it's temporary UI chrome, hooks are fine.

### Why This Matters for MMS
The MMS application has complex state that touches many features:
- **Fleet data**: Displayed in canvas, edit forms, schedule views
- **PM scheduling**: Affects canvas rendering, constraint validation, risk visualization
- **Drag-and-drop**: Requires validation, constraint checking, provisional updates
- **Zoom/pan**: Affects what gets rendered, semantic zoom behavior

All of this state must be centralized in machines to:
1. Ensure consistency across features
2. Enable constraint validation (can't violate PM timing)
3. Support undo/redo and provisional updates
4. Provide time-travel debugging
5. Make the application testable

---

## 7. Key Takeaways for Documentation

### Why State Machines Win (Summary)
1. **Explicit over implicit**: All possible states and transitions are declared upfront
2. **Centralized over scattered**: Single machine is the authoritative source
3. **Visualizable**: Machines can be diagrammed for stakeholder alignment
4. **Debuggable**: Time-travel and deterministic state transitions
5. **Testable**: Given event E in state S, result is always deterministic
6. **Impossible state prevention**: FSM structure prevents invalid combinations

### The Real Cost of Hooks at Scale
1. **Cognitive load**: Must read entire codebase to understand state space
2. **Maintenance burden**: Refactoring is dangerous (prop structure changes break things)
3. **Bug factory**: Invalid state combinations and stale state in different parts of app
4. **Debugging nightmare**: No centralized view of state changes or transitions
5. **Retrainment**: New developers must learn entire state scatter to contribute

### MMS Philosophy
- **State machines first**: All application-level state
- **Hooks for UI chrome**: Only temporary, local UI state
- **@belt/tools as standard**: Consistent patterns across all features
- **Testability as first-class**: All state machines are designed for deterministic testing

---

## References

### State Machines vs Hooks
- [The modern guide to React state patterns - LogRocket Blog](https://blog.logrocket.com/modern-guide-react-state-patterns/)
- [State Machines in React](https://mastery.games/post/state-machines-in-react/)
- [React Without the State Machine - Medium](https://medium.com/eureka-engineering/react-without-the-state-machine-ce4fd25a6180)

### Redux vs useState

- [State Management Beyond useState - Medium](https://medium.com/@karanssoni2002/state-management-beyond-usestate-context-api-vs-redux-vs-zustand-vs-jotai-ee6627b45d1b)
- [React State Management: When to Use useState vs. Redux - Medium](https://medium.com/@mayank2000jain/react-state-management-when-to-use-usestate-vs-redux-e556886de4cb)

### XState Philosophy
- [XState - JavaScript State Machines and Statecharts](https://xstate.js.org/)
- [Empowering Frontend Applications with FSM and XState - Devspedia](https://devspedia.com/empowering-frontend-applications-with-fsm-and-xstate/)
- [Guidelines for State Machines and XState - Kyle Shevlin](https://kyleshevlin.com/guidelines-for-state-machines-and-xstate/)

### Single Source of Truth
- [What is a Single Source of Truth (SSOT)?](https://www.getguru.com/reference/single-source-of-truth)
- [Single Source of Truth Guide - Lucid](https://lucid.co/blog/single-source-of-truth-guide)

### Hooks Problems
- [Understanding Prop Drilling and How to Solve It - DEV Community](https://dev.to/paulocappa/understanding-prop-drilling-and-how-to-avoid-it-2bnd)
- [Meet Reactime — A Time-traveling State Debugger for React - Medium](https://medium.com/@yujinkay/meet-reactime-a-time-traveling-state-debugger-for-react-24f0fce96802)

# State Machines vs React Hooks: Architectural Philosophy & Research

**Research Date:** January 14, 2026
**Purpose:** Document the architectural rationale for blocking hooks in complex applications and using state machines as the single source of truth.

---

## Executive Summary

State machines (FSMs) and statecharts represent a fundamentally different architectural philosophy from React hooks for managing complex application state. While hooks are appropriate for local, component-specific state, they create systematic problems in applications with complex, interconnected state transitions. State machines solve these by:

1. **Centralizing state logic** into explicit, visualizable machines
2. **Preventing invalid state combinations** through finite state constraints
3. **Enabling time-travel debugging** and state reproducibility
4. **Providing a single source of truth** for all application behavior
5. **Facilitating cross-team communication** (developers, designers, stakeholders)

---

## 1. State Machines vs React Hooks: Core Philosophical Differences

### React Hooks Philosophy
- **Implicit state machines**: Each component's state logic is scattered across multiple hooks, effects, and conditional branches
- **Local by default**: Each hook manages state independently without global coordination
- **Coupled to components**: State logic is tightly bound to React component lifecycle
- **Difficult mental model**: No centralized picture of "what states can the app be in?" or "what are all valid transitions?"

### State Machines Philosophy
- **Explicit state machines**: All possible states and transitions are declared upfront
- **Centralized**: Single machine is the authoritative source for application behavior
- **Framework agnostic**: State logic is independent of UI framework (React, Vue, Svelte, etc.)
- **Clear mental model**: Developers and stakeholders can visualize the entire state space

**Sources:**
- [The modern guide to React state patterns - LogRocket Blog](https://blog.logrocket.com/modern-guide-react-state-patterns/)
- [State Machines in React](https://mastery.games/post/state-machines-in-react/)

---

## 2. Why Hooks Fail at Scale: The Real Problems

### Problem 1: Scattered State Logic (No Single Picture)
When using hooks, state logic is distributed across:
- Multiple `useState()` calls in a component
- Logic in `useEffect()` hooks
- Conditional branches in event handlers
- State synchronization scattered across multiple components

**The Problem**: To understand what states the application can be in, you must:
1. Read every component's state definitions
2. Trace all useEffect dependencies
3. Map out all possible conditional branches
4. Mentally model all edge cases

This is exponentially harder as complexity grows.

**State Machine Alternative**: All states and transitions are declared in one place:
```typescript
const machine = createMachine({

    loading: { on: { IMPORT_COMPLETE: 'viewing' } },
    viewing: { on: { DRAG_START: 'dragging', ZOOM: 'viewing' } },
    dragging: { on: { DROP: 'validating', CANCEL: 'viewing' } },
    validating: { on: { VALID: 'viewing', INVALID: 'error' } },
    error: { on: { RETRY: 'viewing' } },
  },

```

Developers, designers, and stakeholders can immediately see all 5 states and all valid transitions.

**Sources:**
- [React Without the State Machine](https://medium.com/eureka-engineering/react-without-the-state-machine-ce4fd25a6180)
- [Selecting a finite state machine library for React](https://www.rainforestqa.com/blog/selecting-a-finite-state-machine-library-for-react)

### Problem 2: Prop Drilling & Hidden Dependencies

1. Lifting state up to common ancestor
2. Threading state through all intermediate components as props
3. Creating hidden dependencies that break when component structure changes

**The Result**: Props become cluttered, intermediate components become "pass-through" components, and refactoring the component tree becomes dangerous.

**State Machine Alternative**: All state lives in one machine. Components query what they need. No prop threading required. Refactoring component structure has no impact on state flow.

**Sources:**
- [Understanding Prop Drilling and How to Solve It - DEV Community](https://dev.to/paulocappa/understanding-prop-drilling-and-how-to-avoid-it-2bnd)
- [Redux vs useState: Choosing the Right State Management Tool](https://medium.com/@surenpoghosian/usestate-vs-redux-choosing-the-right-state-management-tool-for-your-react-application-e1a00e8e6ac8)

### Problem 3: Invalid State Combinations
With hooks, it's possible to create impossible states:

```typescript

```

**The Result**: Bugs in edge cases where two state variables get out of sync.

**State Machine Alternative**: Only one state is active at any time. Invalid combinations are mathematically impossible:

```typescript

    idle: {},

    success: {},
    error: {},
  },

// State machine guarantees only ONE of these can be active
```

- [React State Management: When to Use useState vs. Redux](https://medium.com/@mayank2000jain/react-state-management-when-to-use-usestate-vs-redux-e556886de4cb)

### Problem 4: No Time-Travel Debugging
With hooks, debugging complex state flows is painful:
- No record of state transitions over time
- Hard to reproduce bugs (require exact sequence of user actions)
- No way to "rewind" and see what changed
- Debugging across multiple components is a guessing game

**State Machine Alternative**: Every state transition is explicit and recorded. Time-travel debugging tools can:
- Rewind to any previous state
- Replay user actions
- Inspect what caused each transition

### Core XState Philosophy
> "Finite state machines provide a robust framework to manage complex application states, and by leveraging XState, developers can build frontend systems that are predictable, testable, and easier to maintain."

1. **Predictability**: All possible states are known in advance
2. **Testability**: State transitions are deterministic (given event E in state S, result is always deterministic)

### XState as Stakeholder Communication Tool

This is particularly valuable for complex applications like MMS where:

- QA needs to verify all state combinations

- [XState - JavaScript State Machines and Statecharts](https://xstate.js.org/)

- [Guidelines for State Machines and XState](https://kyleshevlin.com/guidelines-for-state-machines-and-xstate/)

---

## 5. Redux vs useState: The Deeper Lesson

### Why Redux Won't Die
Redux introduced patterns that are now standard in complex apps:
1. **Centralized store**: Single source of truth
2. **Predictable mutations**: Actions describe what happened, reducers describe how state changes
3. **Middleware ecosystem**: Handles async operations, logging, analytics
4. **Developer tools**: Redux DevTools for time-travel debugging
5. **Organizational clarity**: Clear data flow (action → reducer → state → UI)

### Why useState Alone Is Insufficient
While useState is simple for local state, it fails at scale because:
1. **No centralization**: Each component manages its own state

4. **No debugging tools**: No time-travel, no middleware, no tooling

The best pattern for complex apps combines:
- **useState**: Only for truly local UI state (form input, hover state, etc.)
- **State machines**: For application-level state and complex workflows
- **Redux/Zustand**: Alternative to state machines if you prefer action-reducer pattern

- [State Management Beyond useState: Context API vs Redux vs Zustand vs Jotai](https://medium.com/@karanssoni2002/state-management-beyond-usestate-context-api-vs-redux-vs-zustand-vs-jotai-ee6627b45d1b)

- **Testability as first-class**: All state machines are designed for deterministic testing

### Redux vs useState
- [useState vs. Redux: Choosing the Right State Management Tool - Medium](https://medium.com/@surenpoghosian/usestate-vs-redux-choosing-the-right-state-management-tool-for-your-react-application-e1a00e8e6ac8)
- [State Management Beyond useState - Medium](https://medium.com/@karanssoni2002/state-management-beyond-usestate-context-api-vs-redux-vs-zustand-vs-jotai-ee6627b45d1b)

### XState Philosophy
- [XState - JavaScript State Machines and Statecharts](https://xstate.js.org/)
- [Empowering Frontend Applications with FSM and XState - Devspedia](https://devspedia.com/empowering-frontend-applications-with-fsm-and-xstate/)

### Single Source of Truth
- [What is a Single Source of Truth (SSOT)?](https://www.getguru.com/reference/single-source-of-truth)

### Hooks Problems

content.text:
Perfect! I've completed comprehensive research on state machines vs React hooks and created a detailed synthesis document. Here's the summary:

## Research Completed: State Machine Philosophy

I've researched all five topics and compiled findings into `/Users/anotonio.silva/.claude/plans/state-machine-philosophy-research.md`. Here are the key findings:

### Core Architectural Insights

**1. State Machines Solve Real Scale Problems**
- **Explicit vs Implicit**: Hooks scatter state logic across components (implicit machines). State machines declare all states and transitions upfront (explicit), making the entire application state space visible at a glance.
- **Scattered State Logic**: With hooks, understanding "what states can the app be in?" requires reading multiple components, tracing useEffect dependencies, and mentally mapping all edge cases. State machines put all this in one place.
- **Single Source of Truth**: State machines centralize all application state, preventing the prop drilling and sync issues that plague hook-based architectures.

**2. Why Redux's Philosophy Still Applies**
Redux introduced patterns (centralized store, predictable mutations, DevTools, time-travel debugging) that remain the gold standard because they solve real problems:
- Centralization prevents sync issues
- Middleware enables sophisticated side effects
- Time-travel debugging is invaluable
- Clear data flow (action → reducer → state → UI)

useState alone fails at scale because it provides none of these.

**3. XState's Stakeholder Communication Value**
State machines aren't just for developers—they're communication tools:
- Machines can be diagrammed and understood by designers, PMs, and QA
- They serve as "signed-off specifications" before implementation
- They make onboarding new developers trivial (just read the machine diagram)
- They enable cross-team alignment on all possible workflows

**4. Hooks Problems at Scale**
- **Prop Drilling**: Passing state through 5+ intermediate components
- **Invalid State Combinations**: Multiple independent booleans can create impossible states
- **No Debugging**: No time-travel, no transition history, no tooling
- **Hidden Dependencies**: Component tree refactoring breaks state flow

**5. MMS-Specific Application**
For the MMS application, state machines are essential because:
- Fleet data affects multiple features (canvas, forms, views)
- PM scheduling requires constraint validation
- Drag-and-drop needs provisional updates and validation
- Risk visualization depends on consistent fleet state
- All state must be centralized for debugging complex workflows

### Rule for MMS Documentation
**Application-level state** (anything that affects multiple components, persists across renders, or represents business logic) MUST use @belt/tools state machines. Hooks are acceptable ONLY for temporary UI chrome (input focus, hover states, animation phases).

The document is ready for use in architectural documentation and team guidelines.

Sources:
- [The modern guide to React state patterns - LogRocket Blog](https://blog.logrocket.com/modern-guide-react-state-patterns/)
- [State Machines in React](https://mastery.games/post/state-machines-in-react/)
- [XState - JavaScript State Machines and Statecharts](https://xstate.js.org/)
- [Redux vs useState comparison - Medium](https://medium.com/@surenpoghosian/usestate-vs-redux-choosing-the-right-state-management-tool-for-your-react-application-e1a00e8e6ac8)
